bool possibleBipartition(int n, vector<vector<int>>& dislikes) {
    vector<vector<int>> adj(n+1);
    for (const auto& d : dislikes) {
        adj[d[0]].push_back(d[1]);
        adj[d[1]].push_back(d[0]);
    }
    vector<int> color(n+1, -1);
    for (int i=1; i<=n; ++i) {
        if (color[i] == -1) {
            queue<int> q;
            q.push(i);
            color[i] = 0;
            while (!q.empty()) {
                int u = q.front();
                int current_color = color[u];
                q.pop();
                for (int v : adj[u]) {
                    if (color[v] == current_color) {
                        return false;
                    }
                    if (color[v] == -1) {
                        color[v] = 1 - current_color;
                        q.push(v);
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
    return true;
}

class Solution {
public:
    bool possibleBipartition(int n, vector<vector<int>>& dislikes) {
        vector<vector<int>> adj(n+1);
        for (const auto& d : dislikes) {
            adj[d[0]].push_back(d[1]);
            adj[d[1]].push_back(d[0]);
        }
        vector<int> color(n+1, -1);
        for (int i=1; i<=n; ++i) {
            if (color[i] == -1) {
                queue<int> q;
                q.push(i);
                color[i] = 0;
                while (!q.empty()) {
                    int u = q.front();
                    int current_color = color[u];
                    q.pop();
                    for (int v : adj[u]) {
                        if (color[v] == current_color) {
                            return false;
                        }
                        if (color[v] == -1) {
                            color[v] = 1 - current_color;
                            q.push(v);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
};

solution: 
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <queue>
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    bool possibleBipartition(int n, vector<vector<int>>& dislikes) {
        vector<vector<int>> adj(n + 1);
        for (const auto& d : dislikes) {
            adj[d[0]].push_back(d[1]);
            adj[d[1]].push_back(d[0]);
        }
        vector<int> color(n + 1, -1);
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) {
            if (color[i] == -1) {
                queue<int> q;
                q.push(i);
                color[i] = 0;
                while (!q.empty()) {
                    int u = q.front();
                    int current_color = color[u];
                    q.pop();
                    for (int v : adj[u]) {
                        if (color[v] == current_color) {
                            return false;
                        }
                        if (color[v] == -1) {
                            color[v] = 1 - current_color;
                            q.push(v);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(N + E) where N is the number of people and E is the number of dislike relationships
space complexity: O(N + E) for adjacency list and color array
energy implications: LOW
explanation: This solution models the problem as a graph bipartition check using BFS. Each person is a node, and dislikes form undirected edges. The BFS colors nodes with alternating colors, ensuring no two connected nodes share the same color. Key strengths include linear time/space complexity and handling disconnected components. The main weakness is that it may not scale efficiently for extremely large graphs beyond the problem's constraints.
